track_listing.details: LP-Can't Argue With A Sick Mind
Into The Black; LP-Rust Never Sleeps
LP-Herman Brood And His Wild Romance

track_listing.track: Shine On You Crazy Diamond Pts 1 to 5

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Thomas the Vance here, and once again can I welcome you to the Friday Rock Show.
And tonight we feature the first of the live recordings made during the Reading Festival last weekend.
In the coming weeks you can hear Steve Hackett, Molly Hatchet, Climax Blues Band, Gillan and Whitesnake.
And tonight for 41 minutes uninterrupted, the Scorpions.
We've got classic cuts by The Who and a long one by the Pink Floyd and some other people as well.
we've got new releases from The A's,
Gary Newman, Van Morrison, Herman Brood, and ACDC.
Also, we have the Friday Night Connection
to titillate your imagination.
Tonight, we start with Joe Walsh.
And that's a really good new single that's out at the moment
by a band called The A's.
Don't know much about them, believe them to be American.
It's a new single called After Last Night on Aristo.
And prior to that, something which is a real classic
and always goes down well both in the living room,
the bedroom, and on the radio
by Joe Walsh, Rocky Mountain Way.
The album Can't Argue With A Sick Mind
came out in 1976.
Played especially for Kevin P. Duffy
of Barnby Moor, I think it is up there in York.
And also for a gentleman who's off to Canada
for six months on September the 4th
with a lot of love
from a lady by the name of Angie McDowell.
And that gentleman's name, obviously, is Tony Walsh.
Tony, have a good time over there in Canada.
You'll enjoy it. It's a good country.
And Angie, I know you like Nazareth. Stay tuned.
5.15 by The Who from Quadrophenia.
And I played it because I was accosted recently
by a gentleman by the name of Tommy Vance
who told me a sad story about one night
when he was feeling a bit low and depressed,
he actually dropped into his neighbourhood cinema,
paid his one and ninepence,
expecting to be thoroughly ripped off
with this film called Quadrophenia
that they're providing the music for
and they are executive producers for.
And he thought it was going to be a bit of a cheap film,
but this strange character by the name of Tommy Vance
told me that he thought it was one of the best British films
that he'd seen in years.
And before that, something from the new album by Gary Neumann.
The album's called The Pleasure Principle,
and it looks like Gary Neumann is going to possibly achieve,
And, well, quite a good record, I would suggest,
because at the moment he's got two LPs in the top 30.
If he gets the Pleasure Principle in as well,
he'll have three and a few bob in the bank.
It's now 10.22 on the Friday Rock Show.
Well, tomorrow sees the big day out in Edinburgh,
and this man tops the bill.
And that was Neil Young, his latest album.
The shank was hey, hey, my, my, into the black,
and I splayed it, splayed it as well,
especially for everybody who I met in a place called The Liggin' up there in Reading,
particularly a gentleman by the name of Tim
and another gentleman by the name of Ian Meikle, who likes Neil Young.
Also got a letter from a gentleman who's got a son in a band called The Purple Hearts.
The gentleman describes himself as a middle-aged rock fan
who is extremely fond of Neil Young,
and that is RG of No. 185 Main Road, Gideon Park in Romford in Essex.
Hope it's suited.
And before that, you heard something from the new album by Van Morrison,
whose birthday it is today.
Van Morrison, happy birthday to you, pal.
He doesn't talk about that anymore, does he?
The track was Bright Side of the Road.
As I said, Van Morrison is going to be headlining tomorrow
at the Big Day Out in Edinburgh,
just in case you aren't sort of familiar with that part of the world.
Let me tell you, the Big Day Out is going to be actually at Inglestown,
which is just outside Edinburgh.
It's on the A8 or the M8,
depending upon which direction you're coming from,
between Glasgow and Edinburgh.
and for those travelling overnight, there is a free campsite.
Now, tomorrow there will be a continuous bus service from the centre of Edinburgh.
There's parking space up there too for something like 20,000 cars.
And this is nice.
People under 10 who are with an adult can get in for free.
And besides the music, during the day there's going to be over 100 entertainers
like folk dancers and mime artists, puppets, shows, street theatre shows
and general all-round family entertainment.
Tell you who's on the bill later.
All worth a perusal, that album.
It's by Herman Brood and his Wild Romance,
and the track you heard there was called Saturday Night.
And before that, ACDC, and I was walking all over you, actually,
the track from Highway to Hell, and we played it for a load of people,
including Zippy and Boot and Gibson and Jim and Mole and Frank
and Jim and Shona and Caroline and Alison,
and everybody at the Seaforth Athletics Club, especially TG,
and all in the fourth year at Belmont Academy in Ayrshire.
It looks like I've run out of my Radio 1 theme by Van de Graaff Generator,
unfortunately, but there you go.
I'll still continue. We also played ACDC for Chris Naylor of Ainsdale in Southport,
and for Robert Duhamel, who wants to find a lady by the name of Hannah Ramsey,
last known address somewhere down in Cornwall.
If Hannah Ramsey, whom he thinks is living in Sussex, is listening,
if you'd like to write to me, I'll give you Mr. Robert Duhamel's current address.
OK, let's get on now to the Friday Night Connection, without the theme that usually runs behind.
Now, the winner this week is a gentleman by the name of Kevin Campbell of No. 6,
Berks plays Lannik in Strathclyde, and he said,
I think the Friday Night Connection answer is four.
And he's right, because the tracks that we played were
C.S.N.Y.'s Southern Man, that's, of course, from Four Way Street,
Billy Cobham, Quadrant Four, and that's from the album called Spectrum,
and Led Zeppelin, Black Dog, and that's from the album,
which was their fourth one.
So, indeed, the answer is four.
Kevin, congratulations.
We'll be sending you a £10 record token,
and I'm happy to say that you've put together your own Friday Night Connection,
which is good, but it's tricky.
There are two ways of looking at it.
So if you think you've got the answer, think again.
It might not be the right one.
Thank you, Van de Graaff.
And that is our Friday Night Connection for this week.
In somewhere in those three bits of music,
there is a very definite connection.
It was put together by Kevin Campbell of No. 6,
Berks Place, Lanark, Strathclyde in Scotland.
Now, I think I ought to give you just a little bit of a clue.
It's not the obvious answer.
Not the obvious answer.
If you fancy your chances, just put down your answer on a postcard back of an envelope.
Any way you like.
Any communication you care to get together, we'll accept.
And send it to Tommy Vance, the Friday Night Connection,
Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London W1A 4WW, okay?
Don't forget to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
And if you're lucky, you might win yourself a £10 record token.
New crazy diamond.
And there are many, many people that have written in to hear that track,
so we played you 13 minutes and 15 seconds of it.
Let me tell you what's happening at the big day out near Edinburgh at Ingolston tomorrow.
The weather, incidentally, overnight, mist or fog and occasional rain.
Weather tomorrow starting dry with sunny clouds, but some rain later.
The expected temperature tomorrow, round about a high of 68 degrees Fahrenheit.
That's 19 or 20 degrees centigrade.
On the bill, starting at one o'clock, The Cheaters, followed by The Valves, then Dick Gawkin, then The Chieftains, Steel Pulse, The Undertones, Squeeze, Talking Heads, and Van Morrison.
And incidentally, The Talking Heads and Van Morrison, it's their only British gig.
That's at the big day out at Ingleston near Edinburgh tomorrow.
I hope you have a really, I suppose I should say, bonny time.
One of the tracks that people say is one of the best the man ever made.
Jimmy Hendrix and Purple Hay, especially for Boffo and Black and Bilbo Wedler, I think it is,
Bozo Humes, Bingo Army Picks, B.E. Wilson,
Bilberry, is that Blueberry?
And last but not least, Jimmy.
And also for Aussie of number eight, Holton Green, Blutton, Stoke-on-Trent in Staffordshire.
Before that, something from an album coming up very shortly called Top Priority.
It features Rory Gallagher, and it's going to come out on Chrysalis.
The track was Public Enemy, number one.
Which is Public Friend, number one.
Okay, it was last Saturday night at Reading.
There was myself and a few other people sitting in the Manor Mobile
watching colour television at the time.
On the stage were the Scorpions,
and Mr Tony Wilson and his magic fingers were at the ready.
And we sat there for a few minutes in trepidation,
then the lights went up, and then the music started.
The Scorpions in concert.
For the session there was Mike Robinson.
It was mixed by Bill Aitken, and it was produced by Tony Wilson.
And it sounded pretty good in the studio.
I would think equally as amazing at home.
Don't forget Mike Reed and the Peeler,
Monday to Thursday on Radio 1,
on Rock On Tomorrow,
interviews with Jimmy Percy
on why he's rejoined Shan 69.
Loudon Wainwright will be having a chat with us,
and Richard Skinner is presenting a feature
on the Star Revival.
Our engineer tonight on the Friday Rock Show
has been John.
Thank you, John.
Don't forget, Friday Night Connection,
with all those clues you should be getting it,
£20 record token offered.
Wherever you are, take care of yourselves.
I'll see you tomorrow for Rock on at 4 o'clock on Radio 1.
From me, Thomas Vance in London, God bless and good night.
